planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich soil in shade to partial shade; downy maiden fern spreads by spores and short-creeping rhizomes and will naturalize freely in appropriate moist, shaded conditions in zones 8–11.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in zones 8–11; downy maiden fern is one of the most common and adaptable ferns in zone 9–10 Florida gardens, forming lush, bright green colonies in moist, shaded conditions under trees and in streamside settings. The soft, downy texture on the undersides of the fronds gives rise to the common name. Native to tropical Asia, Africa, and the Pacific, it has naturalized across the humid Southeast and behaves as a reliable year-round perennial in frost-free areas, dying back in hard freezes but re-emerging quickly. An excellent filler for wet, shaded spots in tropical and subtropical landscapes where few other groundcovers succeed. Note that this species is considered invasive in Hawaii and in some natural areas — in zone 9 continental Southeast landscapes it is generally well-behaved.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall at a depth of 1–2 inches in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in shade; false Solomon's seal spreads slowly by rhizomes to form loose colonies over time. From seed, expect 2–3 years to bloom.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; false Solomon's seal is a graceful, arching native woodland perennial easily confused with Solomon's seal at a distance, but distinguished by the terminal cluster of tiny, fragrant white flowers at the tip of the stem — rather than the hanging bells along the stem of true Solomon's seal. In late summer the flowers are replaced by clusters of attractive speckled berries that ripen from green to translucent red, consumed by thrushes, veeries, and other woodland birds. The plant spreads slowly by rhizomes to form open, natural-looking colonies that are excellent companions for trilliums, jack-in-the-pulpit, and wild ginger. The foliage turns clear yellow in fall. Formerly classified as Smilacina racemosa.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall 1–2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil; great Solomon's seal is the largest native US species and slowly spreads by rhizomes to form impressive colonies over years. From seed, expect very slow germination requiring double dormancy.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; great Solomon's seal is the largest native North American Polygonatum species, with arching stems that can reach 4–6 feet and broad, parallel-veined leaves arranged alternately along the stem. The hanging, greenish-white tubular flowers in late spring hang in pairs beneath the leaves and attract bumblebees; the blue-black berries that follow are consumed by thrushes and other birds. The bold, arching habit creates a dramatic vertical element in the shade garden and the plant combines superbly with hostas, ferns, and bleeding heart. A slow spreader but long-lived — established colonies in appropriate conditions persist for decades with no care. Excellent for naturalizing in woodland gardens under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic soil in shade; evergreen Solomon's seal is one of the best low-maintenance shade perennials for zones 6–9, combining a beautiful arching habit with year-round foliage in mild winters.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; evergreen Solomon's seal is a Chinese relative of Polygonatum with remarkable ornamental virtues — unlike the deciduous native Solomon's seals, this species retains its lustrous, dark green, arching foliage through winter in zones 7–9, providing year-round structure in the shade garden. The small, white, nodding bell flowers in late spring have a sweet fragrance and the plant spreads slowly by short rhizomes to form an attractive, weed-suppressing groundcover. The combination of shade tolerance, evergreen habit, and adaptability to the heat and humidity of zone 8–9 makes it particularly valuable for Southern shade gardens where deciduous perennials disappear for half the year. Spreads slowly and does not become aggressive.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic woodland soil in shade; Indian cucumber root grows in deep shade under mature deciduous trees and should never be collected from the wild. The plant takes several years to establish from rhizome.

directions: Plant in deep, moist, humus-rich, acidic woodland soil in full shade; Indian cucumber root is a delicate native wildflower of eastern North American forests, easily recognized by its two distinct whorls of leaves — a lower whorl of 5–9 leaves and an upper whorl of 3 leaves from which small, nodding, greenish-yellow flowers hang in early summer, followed by shiny dark purple berries. The edible part is the small tuber that smells and tastes distinctly of cucumber — eaten raw as a trail snack by Indigenous peoples across its range and historically by European settlers. It is a plant of undisturbed, mature, moist woodland with deep leaf litter, and is extremely slow to establish. Never collect from the wild. The darkening of the upper leaves and bracts to purplish at fruiting time is a distinctive recognition trait.
